Our purpose is to strengthen people's trust in the law and this does
guide, uh, what we do every day. Uh, this year
we refreshed our statement of intent, uh, to provide a clearer view of our longer
term outcomes and priorities for the next four years, uh, to
ensure alignment with change in government priorities and outline
specific activities to achieve those outcomes and
priorities. Uh, our updated statement of intent identifies three
outcomes we believe define a just soc- society from, uh, our
perspective. Firstly, uh, trusted and legitimate, where people
believe in the fairness and independence of the law.
Uh, secondly, a justice system that is accessible and effective, where
justice isn't delayed and where everyone, regardless of means, can
participate meaningfully. And thirdfl- uh, thirdly, a
safe and prosperous society where people feel secure and the law
supports both social cohesion and economic
confidence. Overall, these key strategic objectives remain
consistent with what we've been working towards over the past five years.
And they're not abstract ideas. We want them to be lived realities for
all New Zealanders. We've had a really busy year in the
ministry supporting the government's focus on the justice system and law and
order, uh, noting its recent announcement that it is tracking
well ahead of the, uh, violent... victims of violent crime
target, reducing that. Uh, we're also working hard to improve timeliness
in the courts and improve our digital and physical infrastructure, and I'm
sure we'll talk more about that this afternoon.
Uh, and obviously we are managing in a constrained fiscal environment and that
does color quite a lot of our world.
Alongside our justice partners, uh, and the judiciary, we are tackling court
backlogs, recognizing the real effect that these have on victims,
defendants, and other parties. Uh, the national
backlog of criminal cases in the District Court, uh, has fallen by
27% since February '24, which is the equivalent of
around 2,200 cases. Uh, active coronial
cases have decreased by 19% in the year to October, uh, from
five and a half thousand to four and a half thousand cases.
In the Disputes Tribunal, an extra 1,200 were completed in the 12 months to
October compared to the previous year.
Uh, these examples show we can make progress on delays and backlogs.
It's felt like an intractable challenge at times, but we're, uh, really, I think,
demonstrating that we can get some traction, uh, in
it. But none of it is achieved in isolation.
It's a testament to the collective will of everyone involved.
Uh, we've also ramped up our fines and reparations collection effort.
In '24-'25, uh, $223 million
was collected. That's 50 dol- $50 million more than in
2022, uh, '23. And as a result of that,
outstanding reparation money owed to victims has dropped from, uh,
111.8 million in the year to June '24 to 104.6
million this year. Our policy team has been incredibly
busy. We've supported the government with sentencing reform, reinstated the three
strikes regime, we worked on coward punch offending, intro- introduced a new
stalking offense. Measures to tackle retail crime have been progressed
following advice from the Ministerial Advisory Group for Victims of Retail
Crime. On cybercrime, we've worked to ensure New Zealand meets the requirements of
the Budapest Convention, and we're currently leading an overhaul of the
country's anti-money laundering and countering the financing of
terrorism system.
Two phases, (clears throat) excuse me, of the government's firearms reform program
have been delivered, improvements to firearms
prohibition orders and rule changes for shooting clubs and
ranges. Work is advancing on the Arms Act rewrite, and we're helping to
move the Firearms Safety Authority to a new independent regulatory
agency. In the civil and family law space, we have ensured small
straightforward estates can be distributed without needing High Court approval
for probate. We've strengthened the Family Court's ability to address litigation
abuse, and we've supported the government to pass urgent legislation
temporarily suspending unsafe overseas adoptions.
We supported this committee's consideration of the Treaty Principles Bill,
including analysis of an unprecedented volume of public submissions,
and we've developed options to improve electoral system operations.
On the infrastructure front, we're tackling long-standing issues with both our
digital and physical infrastructure.
We're replacing the court's, uh, paper-based system with a digital case management
system, uh, Te Oreka, uh, and we're currently in the middle,
uh, or nearing the end of the build phase for the Family Court.
And there's an extensive property work program underway with several major
rebuilds and other significant projects.
Uh, we've just finished consulting and are about to finalize our latest Long Term

services. It would be good to have an opportunity to discuss that with this
committee, uh, in the new year, if you desire

on a regular basis. And-
